questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of dry mouth, blurred vision, and difficulty climbing stairs. On examination, he has mild ptosis, proximal muscle weakness in legs, and depressed tendon reflexes that increase after sustained contraction. He is a heavy smoker. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is characterized by proximal weakness, autonomic symptoms (dry mouth, blurred vision), and the pathognomonic finding of reflex facilitation after sustained contraction. In contrast, myasthenia gravis typically has ocular and bulbar prominence without reflex changes, and botulism presents with acute descending paralysis.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with a history of ovarian cancer treated 2 years ago presents with a 2-month history of a red rash around her eyes and on her knuckles. She notes mild fatigue but no muscle weakness. Examination reveals heliotrope discolouration of the eyelids and erythematous papules over the metacarpophalangeal and interphalangeal joints. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The heliotrope rash and Gottron's papules are pathognomonic for dermatomyositis, even in the absence of muscle weakness. SLE malar rash spares the nasolabial folds and does not affect knuckles; rosacea is central facial; eczema herpeticum is vesicular; contact dermatitis is pruritic and lacks the specific distribution.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known COPD presents with confusion and lethargy. He has nausea and vomiting for 3 days. Medications include inhaled bronchodilators and a thiazide diuretic. Serum sodium 118 mmol/L, serum osmolality 250 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 450 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 60 mmol/L. He is a heavy smoker. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion
questions.explanation: SIADH is confirmed by hyponatraemia, low plasma osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine, and high urinary sodium (>20). Thiazide-induced hyponatraemia usually has low urinary sodium; adrenal insufficiency often has hyperkalaemia; hypothyroidism causes constipation and bradycardia; psychogenic polydipsia leads to maximally dilute urine.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with rapidly progressive hypertension, weight gain, and easy bruising over 6 weeks. She has proximal muscle weakness and purple striae on her abdomen. Bloods: K+ 3.0 mmol/L, glucose 12 mmol/L, metabolic alkalosis. ACTH >200 pg/mL, cortisol unsuppressed by high-dose dexamethasone. CT chest shows a 3 cm right hilar mass. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Ectopic ACTH presents with rapid onset, severe hypokalaemia, very high ACTH, and lack of suppression on high-dose dexamethasone, often due to small cell lung cancer. Cushing's disease typically is slower with mild ACTH elevation and suppressible cortisol; adrenal adenoma suppresses ACTH; exogenous use has history; pseudo-Cushing's has normal suppression.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of prostate cancer (treated with radiotherapy 5 years ago, in remission) presents with 2-month progressive proximal muscle weakness, dysphagia, a pinkish-red heliotrope rash around his eyes, and erythematous plaques over his knuckles. No recent infections. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The heliotrope rash and Gottron's papules are diagnostic of dermatomyositis, which is frequently paraneoplastic and can herald recurrence or new malignancy even with a remote cancer history. Polymyositis has no rash; inclusion body myositis affects distal muscles and older patients; muscular dystrophy is lifelong; statin-induced myopathy resolves with drug cessation.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with known small cell lung cancer presents with proximal muscle weakness, fatigability, and areflexia. Electromyography shows increment >100% following high-frequency repetitive stimulation. Which laboratory test is most likely to confirm the paraneoplastic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) is associated with small cell lung cancer and antibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCC). Anti-AChR is for myasthenia gravis, anti-MuSK for seronegative MG, anti-VGKC for limbic encephalitis, and striational for thymoma.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a pelvic mass presents with subacute onset of ataxia, dysarthria, and nystagmus. MRI brain is unremarkable. Cerebrospinal fluid shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis. Which serological test is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration in women with gynaecological or breast cancer is typically associated with anti-Yo (anti-Purkinje cell) antibodies. Anti-Hu is more common with lung cancer, anti-Ri with opsoclonus-myoclonus, anti-Tr with Hodgkin lymphoma, and anti-Ma2 with testicular germ cell tumours.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of heavy smoking presents with insidious onset of personality change, short-term memory loss, and complex partial seizures. MRI brain shows high signal in both medial temporal lobes. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and oligoclonal bands. Which investigation is most specific for the underlying paraneoplastic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Limbic encephalitis in an older smoker is most likely due to small cell lung cancer with anti-Hu (ANNA-1) antibodies. Anti-LGI1 is often non-paraneoplastic, anti-GAD associated with stiff person syndrome, anti-NMDAR with ovarian teratoma (younger females), and anti-CRMP5 is less common.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with a violaceous rash on the eyelids and painful, swollen, red papules on the knuckles. She also has proximal muscle weakness. Creatine kinase is elevated. Which investigation is most appropriate to identify an associated malignancy?
options: CT scan of chest, abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: Dermatomyositis in older patients has a strong association with underlying malignancy. NICE guidelines recommend CT chest/abdomen/pelvis as first-line screening for occult cancer. PET-CT may follow if negative, but CT CAP is the initial investigation.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a neuroendocrine tumour of the small intestine develops episodic flushing, diarrhoea, and wheezing. Which diagnostic test is most specific for the paraneoplastic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Carcinoid syndrome is caused by serotonin secretion; the most specific diagnostic test is elevated urinary 5-HIAA, a serotonin metabolite. Chromogranin A is less specific; octreotide scan localises tumour; liver biopsy is for metastases.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a heavy smoking history presents with proximal muscle weakness, areflexia, and dry mouth. Electromyography shows a decrement on low-frequency stimulation and increment after exercise. CT chest reveals a 3 cm lung mass and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his neuromuscular syndrome?
options: Chemotherapy for underlying small cell lung cancer
questions.explanation: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) is commonly paraneoplastic to small cell lung cancer. Definitive treatment is eradication of the tumour with chemotherapy; symptomatic therapies like amifampridine are second-line or adjunctive.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with recently diagnosed small cell lung cancer has asymptomatic hyponatraemia (serum sodium 128 mmol/L). She is clinically euvolaemic and has normal renal, adrenal, and thyroid function. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Chemotherapy for small cell lung cancer
questions.explanation: Mild, asymptomatic SIADH is initially managed with fluid restriction. Demeclocycline and tolvaptan are second-line. Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known squamous cell lung cancer presents with confusion, nausea, and polydipsia. Corrected serum calcium is 3.5 mmol/L and ECG shows a short QT interval. Which therapy is CONTRAINDICATED in this patient?
questions.explanation: Thiazide diuretics reduce urinary calcium excretion and can worsen hypercalcaemia, so they are contraindicated. The definitive treatment for paraneoplastic hypercalcaemia is intravenous bisphosphonate (e.g., zoledronic acid) after aggressive IV fluids.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with renal cell carcinoma has a haemoglobin of 18 g/dL and haematocrit 0.55. He is asymptomatic apart from mild headache. Which is the most appropriate definitive therapy for his paraneoplastic erythrocytosis?
questions.explanation: Paraneoplastic erythrocytosis due to RCC is driven by tumour-derived erythropoietin. Definitive treatment is surgical removal of the tumour (nephrectomy). Phlebotomy provides temporary relief but does not address the underlying cause.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with small cell lung cancer achieved complete remission after chemotherapy. He continues to have proximal muscle weakness and fatigue. Electromyography shows post-tetanic facilitation consistent with Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome. Which is the most appropriate first-line therapy for his persistent neuromuscular symptoms?
questions.explanation: In persistent LEMS after tumour treatment, amifampridine is the first-line symptomatic therapy. Pyridostigmine is often less effective. Immunosuppression (steroids, azathioprine) is not indicated when the tumour is no longer present.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of progressive confusion, short-term memory loss, and two recent generalised tonic-clonic seizures. She has a 40 pack-year smoking history. Her CT head scan is normal. She is now alert and oriented to person only. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis
questions.explanation: The history of subacute cognitive decline and seizures in a smoker suggests limbic encephalitis, often paraneoplastic (e.g., anti-Hu) from small cell lung cancer. MRI brain is the key initial investigation to show T2-hyperintensity in the mesial temporal lobes, guiding further tests like anti-neuronal antibodies. Starting anticonvulsants (option 0) may be needed but is not the immediate priority before diagnosis. Lumbar puncture (option 1) is useful later for CSF analysis, but MRI should be first to avoid delay. CT chest (option 3) is for tumour search but follows MRI. Aciclovir (option 4) is for viral encephalitis, which is less likely with this subacute picture and normal CT.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with known breast cancer (treated 2 years ago, currently on surveillance) presents with new symmetrical proximal muscle weakness, a heliotrope rash on her eyelids, and Gottron's papules over her knuckles. She has no respiratory symptoms. Routine bloods are normal. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis
Start high-dose oral prednisolone 40 mg daily
Refer to rheumatology for urgent review
Check serum creatine kinase and aldolase
questions.explanation: The presentation is classic for dermatomyositis, which is paraneoplastic in about 25% of cases. The immediate next step, before treatment, is to confirm the diagnosis by checking muscle enzymes (CK, aldolase) to document myositis. Starting steroids (option 0) without biochemical confirmation risks missing other causes and creates a therapeutic test. CT imaging (option 2) is important but not the first step; the patient already has a known cancer, so recurrence must be considered, but enzyme tests are quicker. Rheumatology referral (option 3) is reasonable but not immediate. IVIG (option 4) is reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known thymoma and myasthenia gravis (on pyridostigmine 60 mg four times daily) presents with a 2-day history of worsening dysphagia, slurred speech, and shortness of breath. On examination, she is tachypnoeic (respiratory rate 28/min), using accessory muscles, with SpO2 91% on 4 L oxygen. She has difficulty managing her own secretions. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform arterial blood gas and measure vital capacity
Increase pyridostigmine to 90 mg four times daily
Start intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 5 days
questions.explanation: This is myasthenic crisis with evidence of respiratory failure and bulbar weakness. The single most important immediate action is to secure the airway via endotracheal intubation to prevent aspiration and respiratory arrest. While options such as ABG and vital capacity (option 2) are helpful to assess severity, they should not delay intubation when the patient already has signs of inadequate ventilation and secretion clearance. IVIG (option 0) and neostigmine (option 1) are treatments for myasthenic crisis but are not first-line when the airway is compromised. Increasing pyridostigmine (option 4) may worsen cholinergic crisis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known metastatic small bowel carcinoid tumour (treated with octreotide long-acting release 30 mg monthly) is found to have a new pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border during a routine outpatient review. He is asymptomatic, with normal vital signs. He has had mild flushing and diarrhoea controlled on octreotide. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for urgent surgical valve replacement
Increase octreotide dose to 40 mg monthly
Start a loop diuretic such as furosemide
questions.explanation: Carcinoid heart disease typically causes right-sided valvular lesions (tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis) due to serotonin-induced fibrosis. The new murmur is a red flag, and the immediate next step is echocardiography to assess valve morphology and function. Increasing octreotide (option 0) may help symptoms but does not address valve disease. Surgical referral (option 1) is premature without imaging. Diuretics (option 2) are not indicated in an asymptomatic patient. Urinary 5-HIAA (option 4) is used to monitor disease but is not the first step for a new murmur.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a history of ovarian cancer (treated 5 years ago, in remission) presents with a 2-month history of progressive ataxia, dysarthria, and nystagmus. She has no headache or focal weakness. An MRI brain 1 month ago was normal. She has had previous tests for paraneoplastic antibodies: anti-Yo positive. She is under surveillance with 6-monthly CT scans, last one 3 months ago showing no recurrence. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days
Repeat MRI brain with and without contrast
Arrange CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis urgently
Refer to neurology for consideration of rituximab
questions.explanation: The patient has known paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration (PCD) with anti-Yo antibodies. Worsening ataxia despite remission of cancer raises concern for progression of PCD or a new CNS pathology (e.g., metastasis, infection). The immediate next step is to repeat MRI brain to look for new lesions, especially since the prior MRI was a month ago and symptoms have progressed. Starting steroids (option 1) is empirical but not first-line without excluding other causes. CT for cancer recurrence (option 2) is important but not as immediate as CNS imaging because she had a recent scan. Antibody titre (option 3) often does not correlate with severity. Rituximab (option 4) is a long-term treatment, not an immediate action.

questions.stem: Mr J, 65, known small cell lung cancer, admitted with confusion and vomiting. Bloods: Na 118 mmol/L, K 4.0, urea 4.5, creatinine 60, glucose 5.2. Urine osmolality 600 mOsm/kg, serum osmolality 250 mOsm/kg. What single finding marks this as a medical emergency?
questions.explanation: In SIADH, severe hyponatraemia (<120) with neurological symptoms (confusion, seizures) indicates a medical emergency requiring immediate hypertonic saline; asymptomatic hyponatraemia does not. Vomiting is non-specific, and the lab values confirm SIADH but do not themselves define the emergency.

questions.stem: Mrs K, 72, known squamous cell lung cancer, presents with polydipsia, polyuria, constipation. Adjusted calcium 3.1 mmol/L, phosphate 0.9, PTH low, creatinine 130 μmol/L. Which single finding is most concerning for an imminent life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Severe hypercalcaemia (>3.0 mmol/L) can cause cardiac arrhythmias; a short QT interval on ECG is a warning of electrical instability and risk of ventricular arrhythmia. The calcium level itself is high but not necessarily imminent if asymptomatic; the ECG change signifies immediate danger.

questions.stem: Mr L, 55, presents with hypertension, proximal wasting, easy bruising, and striae. Serum potassium 2.8 mmol/L, ACTH 250 pg/mL, random cortisol 1500 nmol/L. Which single finding requires immediate escalation?
questions.explanation: Severe hypokalaemia (K <3.0 mmol/L) from ectopic ACTH can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias and muscle paralysis, necessitating urgent correction. Hypertension is common but not immediately dangerous at this level; ACTH elevation and striae are diagnostic but not acute emergencies.

questions.stem: Ms T, 68, known small cell lung cancer, presents with proximal leg weakness, areflexia, dry mouth, and fatigue. Which single finding indicates impending respiratory crisis?
questions.explanation: In Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS), respiratory muscle weakness is the most life-threatening complication; dyspnoea on minimal exertion signals impending respiratory failure requiring ICU admission. Ptosis and dysphagia are common but not immediately dangerous alone.

questions.stem: Mrs D, 50, presents with heliotrope rash, Gottron's papules, dysphagia, and proximal weakness MRC 4/5. Which single clinical finding raises suspicion for a rapidly fatal complication?
questions.explanation: In dermatomyositis, rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease is a life-threatening complication; bibasal crackles warrant urgent HRCT and multidisciplinary input. Dysphagia and weakness are typical but not acutely life-threatening; CK elevation indicates muscle inflammation but not respiratory involvement.
